**Masovian Voivodeship** ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *województwo mazowieckie*, pronounced [[vɔjɛˈvut͡stfɔ mazɔˈvjɛt͡skʲɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q809_(pol)-Olaf-wojew%C3%B3dztwo_mazowieckie.wav "File:LL-Q809 (pol)-Olaf-województwo mazowieckie.wav"))[[a]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) is a [voivodeship](/wiki/Voivodeships_of_Poland "Voivodeships of Poland") in east-central [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), containing Poland's capital [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw").

Masovian Voivodeship has an area of 35,579 square kilometres (13,737 sq mi) and had a 2019 population of 5,411,446, making it Poland's largest and most populous province.[[1]](#cite_note-population-1) Its principal cities are [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") (1.783 million) in the center of the [Warsaw metropolitan area](/wiki/Warsaw_metropolitan_area "Warsaw metropolitan area"), [Radom](/wiki/Radom "Radom") (212,230) to the south, [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock") (119,709) to the west, [Siedlce](/wiki/Siedlce "Siedlce") (77,990) to the east, and [Ostrołęka](/wiki/Ostro%C5%82%C4%99ka "Ostrołęka") (52,071) to the north. It borders six other provinces: [Warmian-Masurian](/wiki/Warmian-Masurian_Voivodeship "Warmian-Masurian Voivodeship") to the north, [Podlaskie](/wiki/Podlaskie_Voivodeship "Podlaskie Voivodeship") to the northeast, [Lublin](/wiki/Lublin_Voivodeship "Lublin Voivodeship") to the southeast, [Świętokrzyskie](/wiki/%C5%9Awi%C4%99tokrzyskie_Voivodeship "Świętokrzyskie Voivodeship") (Holy Cross) to the south, [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA_Voivodeship "Łódź Voivodeship") to the southwest, and [Kuyavian–Pomeranian](/wiki/Kuyavian%E2%80%93Pomeranian_Voivodeship "Kuyavian–Pomeranian Voivodeship") to the northwest.

The name of the province recalls the region's traditional name, [Mazovia](/wiki/Mazovia "Mazovia") (in Polish *Mazowsze*, also spelled Masovia), with which it is roughly coterminous. However, the province's southern part, including Radom, historically belonged to [Lesser Poland](/wiki/Lesser_Poland "Lesser Poland"); while [Łomża](/wiki/%C5%81om%C5%BCa "Łomża") with environs, though historically part of Mazovia, is now part of [Podlaskie Voivodeship](/wiki/Podlaskie_Voivodeship "Podlaskie Voivodeship").

Masovian Voivodeship is Poland's prime center of [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), research, education, industry, and [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure").[[7]](#cite_note-aquatherm-warsaw.com-8) It has Poland's lowest unemployment rate and is a very high-income province.[[7]](#cite_note-aquatherm-warsaw.com-8) It is also popular with tourists due to the many historical monuments and its over 20% forested area of [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak").[[8]](#cite_note-9) The province's [Kampinos National Park](/wiki/Kampinos_National_Park "Kampinos National Park") is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [biosphere reserve](/wiki/Biosphere_reserve "Biosphere reserve").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Hogenberg_View_of_Warsaw.jpg/250px-Hogenberg_View_of_Warsaw.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hogenberg_View_of_Warsaw.jpg)

17th-century view of Warsaw

In the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), the territory was inhabited by the [Masovians](/wiki/Masovians "Masovians"), an old [Polish tribe](/wiki/Polish_tribes "Polish tribes"). It formed part of Poland since its establishment in the 10th century, with the then-regional capital [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock") being the capital of Poland from 1079 to 1138. The *Wzgórze Tumskie* ("Cathedral Hill") in Płock with the [Płock Castle](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Castle "Płock Castle") and the [Catholic Cathedral](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Cathedral "Płock Cathedral"), seat of one of the oldest Polish dioceses, est. in 1075, which contains the sarcophagi of a number of [Polish monarchs](/wiki/Polish_monarchs "Polish monarchs"), is listed as a [Historic Monument of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)").[[9]](#cite_note-10) Later, Płock, Warsaw and [Czersk](/wiki/Czersk,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Czersk, Masovian Voivodeship") were medieval ducal seats of the [Piast dynasty](/wiki/Piast_dynasty "Piast dynasty").

In 1505, Radom hosted the session of the [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Sejm of the Kingdom of Poland") (Polish Parliament), which enacted the *[Nihil novi](/wiki/Nihil_novi "Nihil novi")* act, and in the 16th century, Warsaw hosted several sessions of the Sejm,[[10]](#cite_note-11) before King [Sigismund III Vasa](/wiki/Sigismund_III_Vasa "Sigismund III Vasa") moved the Polish capital from [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") to Warsaw in 1596.

Following the late-18th-century [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), the region witnessed several uprisings against foreign rule: the [Kościuszko Uprising](/wiki/Ko%C5%9Bciuszko_Uprising "Kościuszko Uprising") of 1794, the [November Uprising](/wiki/November_Uprising "November Uprising") of 1830–1831, and the [January Uprising](/wiki/January_Uprising "January Uprising") of 1863–1864.

In the [interbellum](/wiki/Interbellum "Interbellum"), the region was part of reborn independent Poland. In 1920, the region was [invaded by Soviet Russia](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War"), but Poland secured its freedom in the victorious [Battle of Warsaw](/wiki/Battle_of_Warsaw_(1920) "Battle of Warsaw (1920)"). The southern part of the current province was rapidly industrialized as part of the [Central Industrial Region](/wiki/Central_Industrial_Region_(Poland) "Central Industrial Region (Poland)") of Poland.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), it was [occupied by Germany](/wiki/Occupation_of_Poland_(1939%E2%80%931945) "Occupation of Poland (1939–1945)"), with the occupiers committing their genocidal policies against [Poles](/wiki/Nazi_crimes_against_the_Polish_nation "Nazi crimes against the Polish nation") and [Jews](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") in the region, with [expulsions](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Poles_by_Nazi_Germany "Expulsion of Poles by Nazi Germany"), massacres of civilians and [prisoners of war](/wiki/German_atrocities_committed_against_Polish_prisoners_of_war "German atrocities committed against Polish prisoners of war"), including at [Ciepielów](/wiki/Ciepiel%C3%B3w_massacre "Ciepielów massacre"), [Śladów](/wiki/%C5%9Alad%C3%B3w_massacre "Śladów massacre"), [Zakroczym](/wiki/Zakroczym_massacre "Zakroczym massacre"), [Ostrów Mazowiecka](/wiki/Ostr%C3%B3w_Mazowiecka_massacre "Ostrów Mazowiecka massacre"), [Palmiry](/wiki/Palmiry_massacre "Palmiry massacre"), [Firlej](/wiki/Firlej,_Radom "Firlej, Radom"), [Skłoby](/wiki/Sk%C5%82oby "Skłoby"), [Nur](/wiki/Nur,_Poland "Nur, Poland"), [Ochota](/wiki/Ochota_massacre "Ochota massacre"), [Wola](/wiki/Wola_massacre "Wola massacre"), and [Lipniak-Majorat](/wiki/Lipniak-Majorat "Lipniak-Majorat"). Germany operated numerous prisons, [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II") camps, the [Treblinka extermination camp](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp"), in which some 700,000–900,000 people were murdered, and several [prisoner-of-war camps](/wiki/German_prisoner-of-war_camps_in_World_War_II "German prisoner-of-war camps in World War II") for Polish, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Military_Internees "Italian Military Internees"), [French](/wiki/French_prisoners_of_war_in_World_War_II "French prisoners of war in World War II"), Soviet, and Romanian prisoners of war.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Masovian Province was created on 1 January 1999, under the [Polish local-government reforms](/wiki/Polish_local_government_reforms "Polish local government reforms") adopted in 1998, out of the former provinces of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw_Voivodeship_(1975%E2%80%931998) "Warsaw Voivodeship (1975–1998)"), [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Voivodeship "Płock Voivodeship"), [Ciechanów](/wiki/Ciechan%C3%B3w_Voivodeship "Ciechanów Voivodeship"), [Ostrołęka](/wiki/Ostro%C5%82%C4%99ka_Voivodeship "Ostrołęka Voivodeship"), [Siedlce](/wiki/Siedlce_Voivodeship "Siedlce Voivodeship"), and [Radom](/wiki/Radom_Voivodeship "Radom Voivodeship").

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main article: [Masovian Voivodeship Sejmik](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship_Sejmik "Masovian Voivodeship Sejmik")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Pa%C5%82ac_Komisji_Rz%C4%85dowej_Przychod%C3%B3w_i_Skarbu_w_Warszawie_2018.jpg/250px-Pa%C5%82ac_Komisji_Rz%C4%85dowej_Przychod%C3%B3w_i_Skarbu_w_Warszawie_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pa%C5%82ac_Komisji_Rz%C4%85dowej_Przychod%C3%B3w_i_Skarbu_w_Warszawie_2018.jpg)

Voivodeship Office in Warsaw

The Masovian voivodeship's government is headed by the province's **voivode** ([governor](/wiki/Voivodes_of_Poland_(since_1999) "Voivodes of Poland (since 1999)")) who is appointed by the [Polish Prime Minister](/wiki/Polish_Prime_Minister "Polish Prime Minister"). The **voivode** is then assisted in performing his duties by the [voivodeship's marshal](/wiki/Voivodeship_marshal "Voivodeship marshal"), who is the appointed speaker for the [voivodeship's executive](/wiki/Voivodeship_executive_board "Voivodeship executive board") and is elected by the **sejmik** ([provincial assembly](/wiki/Voivodeship_sejmik "Voivodeship sejmik")). The current **voivode** of Masovia is [Mariusz Frankowski](/wiki/Mariusz_Frankowski "Mariusz Frankowski").

The Sejmik of Masovia consists of 51 members.

## Historical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical")]

### Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)")]

Further information: [Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)](/wiki/Masovian_Voivodeship_(1526%E2%80%931795) "Masovian Voivodeship (1526–1795)")

**Masovia Voivodeship, 1526–1795** ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Województwo Mazowieckie*) was an administrative region of the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_of_the_Jagiellons "Kingdom of Poland of the Jagiellons"), and of the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), from the 15th century until the [partitions of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland") (1795). Together with [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Voivodeship "Płock Voivodeship") and [Rawa Voivodeships](/wiki/Rawa_Voivodeship "Rawa Voivodeship"), it formed the province ([prowincja](/wiki/Prowincja "Prowincja")) of [Masovia](/wiki/Masovia "Masovia").

### Masovian Voivodeship (1816–1837)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Masovian Voivodeship (1816–1837)")]

Masovian Voivodeship was one of [the voivodeships of Congress Poland](/wiki/Administrative_division_of_Congress_Poland "Administrative division of Congress Poland"). It was formed from the [Warsaw Department](/wiki/Warsaw_Department "Warsaw Department") and transformed into the [Masovia Governorate](/wiki/Masovia_Governorate "Masovia Governorate").

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/316B-002_%286%29%2C_%C5%BBmigr%C3%B3d%2C_2015-12-05.jpg/250px-316B-002_%286%29%2C_%C5%BBmigr%C3%B3d%2C_2015-12-05.jpg)](/wiki/File:316B-002_(6),_%C5%BBmigr%C3%B3d,_2015-12-05.jpg)

[Koleje Mazowieckie](/wiki/Koleje_Mazowieckie "Koleje Mazowieckie") (Masovian Railways)

Three major international road routes pass through the voivodeship: Cork–Berlin–Poznań–Warszawa–Minsk–Moscow–Omsk ([European route E30](/wiki/European_route_E30 "European route E30")), Prague–Wrocław–Warsaw–Białystok–Helsinki ([E67](/wiki/European_route_E67 "European route E67")) and Pskov–Gdańsk–Warsaw–Kraków–Budapest ([E77](/wiki/European_route_E77 "European route E77")).

Currently, there are various stretches of [highways](/wiki/Highways_in_Poland "Highways in Poland") in the area, with the [A2 highway](/wiki/A2_autostrada_(Poland) "A2 autostrada (Poland)") connecting the region, and therefore the capital city, with the rest of Europe. The highway passes directly through the voivodeship from west to east, connecting it with Belarus and Germany. However, the A2 is yet to be built east of Warsaw to connect Poland with Belarus. The [S7 expressway](/wiki/Expressway_S7_(Poland) "Expressway S7 (Poland)") runs through Poland from the north to the south passing through Warsaw, the [S8](/wiki/Expressway_S8_(Poland) "Expressway S8 (Poland)") connects Warsaw with [Białystok](/wiki/Bia%C5%82ystok "Białystok"), in the neighboring north-eastern province, also forming part of the [Via Baltica](/wiki/Via_Baltica "Via Baltica") which heads on to Lithuania, and to [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław") in the south-west, and the [S17](/wiki/Expressway_S17_(Poland) "Expressway S17 (Poland)") being built to connect Warsaw with Lublin in the south-east and on to Ukraine.

The two main railway carriers operating in the region are the regional [Koleje Mazowieckie](/wiki/Koleje_Mazowieckie "Koleje Mazowieckie") and nationwide [PKP Intercity](/wiki/PKP_Intercity "PKP Intercity"). Three of ten busiest railway stations of Poland are located in the voivodeship: [Warszawa Centralna](/wiki/Warszawa_Centralna_railway_station "Warszawa Centralna railway station"), [Warszawa Wschodnia](/wiki/Warszawa_Wschodnia_railway_station "Warszawa Wschodnia railway station"), [Warszawa Zachodnia](/wiki/Warszawa_Zachodnia_railway_station "Warszawa Zachodnia railway station").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

The main international airport in the region is [Warsaw Frederic Chopin Airport](/wiki/Warsaw_Frederic_Chopin_Airport "Warsaw Frederic Chopin Airport").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Economy")]

Masovian Voivodeship is the wealthiest province in Poland. The [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) of the province was PLN 596 billion in 2021, accounting for 22.8% of the Polish economic output. GDP per capita adjusted for purchasing power was around PLN123,000in the same year.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### Unemployment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Unemployment")]

The unemployment rate stood at 4.8% in 2017 and was higher than the national and the European average.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## Sights and tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Sights and tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Castle_Square_%2822346p%29.jpg/250px-Castle_Square_%2822346p%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castle_Square_(22346p).jpg)

[Warsaw Old Town](/wiki/Warsaw_Old_Town "Warsaw Old Town"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") and [Historic Monument of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)")

The top tourist destination of the voivodeship is the capital city of [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw") with its [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_Warsaw "Old Town, Warsaw") and [Royal Castle](/wiki/Royal_Castle,_Warsaw "Royal Castle, Warsaw"), a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") and [Historic Monument of Poland](/wiki/Historic_Monument_(Poland) "Historic Monument (Poland)").[[22]](#cite_note-23) Further Historic Monuments in Warsaw include the [Royal Route](/wiki/Royal_Route,_Warsaw "Royal Route, Warsaw") with several palaces and parks, most notably the [Łazienki Palace](/wiki/%C5%81azienki_Palace "Łazienki Palace") and [Wilanów Palace](/wiki/Wilan%C3%B3w_Palace "Wilanów Palace"), and the [Warsaw Water Filters](/wiki/Warsaw_Water_Filters "Warsaw Water Filters").

Other historic cities include [Radom](/wiki/Radom "Radom") with its old center and parks, [Pułtusk](/wiki/Pu%C5%82tusk "Pułtusk") with the longest paved marketplace of Europe, and [Płock](/wiki/P%C5%82ock "Płock"), former medieval capital of Poland, with its Old Town and *Wzgórze Tumskie* ("Cathedral Hill") with the [Płock Castle](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Castle "Płock Castle") and the [Płock Cathedral](/wiki/P%C5%82ock_Cathedral "Płock Cathedral"), which contains the sarcophagi of a number of Polish monarchs.

There are several medieval castles, including at [Ciechanów](/wiki/Ciechan%C3%B3w_Castle "Ciechanów Castle"), [Czersk](/wiki/Czersk_Castle "Czersk Castle"), [Liw](/wiki/Liw_Castle "Liw Castle"), Płock, and numerous palaces in the voivodeship, including at [Otwock Wielki](/wiki/Bieli%C5%84ski_Palace,_Otwock_Wielki "Bieliński Palace, Otwock Wielki"), [Guzów](/wiki/Soba%C5%84ski_Palace "Sobański Palace"), [Radziejowice](/wiki/Radziejowice_Castle "Radziejowice Castle"), [Krubki-Górki](/wiki/Krubki-G%C3%B3rki "Krubki-Górki"), [Sanniki](/wiki/Sanniki,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Sanniki, Masovian Voivodeship"), [Korczew](/wiki/Korczew,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Korczew, Masovian Voivodeship") and multiple in Warsaw itself. Unique historic churches include the [Temple of Mercy and Charity](/wiki/Temple_of_Mercy_and_Charity "Temple of Mercy and Charity") in Płock, the worldwide headquarters of the [Mariavite Church](/wiki/Mariavite_Church "Mariavite Church"), the [Abbey Church](/wiki/Abbey_Church,_Czerwi%C5%84sk_nad_Wis%C5%82%C4%85 "Abbey Church, Czerwińsk nad Wisłą") in [Czerwińsk nad Wisłą](/wiki/Czerwi%C5%84sk_nad_Wis%C5%82%C4%85 "Czerwińsk nad Wisłą"), one of the best preserved [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture_in_Poland "Romanesque architecture in Poland") [fortified churches](/wiki/Fortified_church "Fortified church") in Poland, and the Saints Roch and John the Baptist church in [Brochów](/wiki/Broch%C3%B3w,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Brochów, Masovian Voivodeship"), a Gothic-Renaissance fortified church, place of baptism of [Fryderyk Chopin](/wiki/Fryderyk_Chopin "Fryderyk Chopin"). [Otwock](/wiki/Otwock "Otwock"), [Józefów](/wiki/J%C3%B3zef%C3%B3w "Józefów") and Warsaw are home to the local [Świdermajer](/wiki/%C5%9Awidermajer "Świdermajer") architectural style. There are also the [Modlin Fortress](/wiki/Modlin_Fortress "Modlin Fortress") and [Warsaw Citadel](/wiki/Warsaw_Citadel "Warsaw Citadel").

The sole [spa town](/wiki/Spa_town "Spa town") of the voivodeship is [Konstancin-Jeziorna](/wiki/Konstancin-Jeziorna "Konstancin-Jeziorna").

There are museums dedicated to composer [Fryderyk Chopin](/wiki/Fryderyk_Chopin "Fryderyk Chopin") and chemist [Marie Curie](/wiki/Marie_Curie "Marie Curie") at their birthplaces in [Żelazowa Wola](/wiki/%C5%BBelazowa_Wola "Żelazowa Wola") and [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), respectively. There is also a [Fryderyk Chopin Museum](/wiki/Fryderyk_Chopin_Museum "Fryderyk Chopin Museum") in Warsaw. There is a museum dedicated to famous [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_in_Poland "Renaissance in Poland") poet [Jan Kochanowski](/wiki/Jan_Kochanowski "Jan Kochanowski") in [Czarnolas](/wiki/Czarnolas,_Zwole%C5%84_County "Czarnolas, Zwoleń County"). The Krasiński Palace in [Opinogóra Górna](/wiki/Opinog%C3%B3ra_G%C3%B3rna "Opinogóra Górna") hosts the Museum of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism").

There are numerous [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") memorials, including memorials at the sites of Nazi massacres of Poles, including [Palmiry](/wiki/Palmiry_massacre "Palmiry massacre"), and [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust") memorials, and museums at the sites of the former Nazi German [Treblinka extermination camp](/wiki/Treblinka_extermination_camp "Treblinka extermination camp"), [Pawiak Prison](/wiki/Pawiak_Prison_Museum "Pawiak Prison Museum") in Warsaw and [Dulag 121 camp in Pruszków](/wiki/Dulag_121_camp_in_Pruszk%C3%B3w "Dulag 121 camp in Pruszków"). Two of the few Italian war cemeteries in Poland are located in Warsaw (from both world wars) and [Nowe Opole](/wiki/Nowe_Opole "Nowe Opole") (from WW2).[[23]](#cite_note-24)

The highest point in the voivodeship, 408-metre (1,339 ft), [Góra Altana](/w/index.php?title=G%C3%B3ra_Altana&action=edit&redlink=1 "Góra Altana (page does not exist)"), is located south of [Szydłowiec](/wiki/Szyd%C5%82owiec "Szydłowiec"), near the southern boundary with the [Świętokrzyskie Voivodeship](/wiki/%C5%9Awi%C4%99tokrzyskie_Voivodeship "Świętokrzyskie Voivodeship").

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg/250px-National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Stadium_Warsaw_aerial_view_2.jpg)

[Kazimierz Górski National Stadium](/wiki/Kazimierz_G%C3%B3rski_National_Stadium "Kazimierz Górski National Stadium") in Warsaw, one of the arenas of the [UEFA Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), [2014 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship](/wiki/2014_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2014 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship"), [2017 Men's European Volleyball Championship](/wiki/2017_Men%27s_European_Volleyball_Championship "2017 Men's European Volleyball Championship") and [Speedway Grand Prix of Poland](/wiki/Speedway_Grand_Prix_of_Poland "Speedway Grand Prix of Poland")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") and [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") enjoy the largest following in the voivodeship. Successful clubs include [Legia Warsaw](/wiki/Legia_Warsaw "Legia Warsaw") and [Polonia Warsaw](/wiki/Polonia_Warsaw "Polonia Warsaw") in football and basketball, and [Wisła Płock](/wiki/Wis%C5%82a_P%C5%82ock_(handball) "Wisła Płock (handball)") in handball.

Since the establishment of the province, several major international sports competitions were co-hosted by the province, including the [2002 World Weightlifting Championships](/wiki/2002_World_Weightlifting_Championships "2002 World Weightlifting Championships"), [2003 World Short Track Speed Skating Championships](/wiki/2003_World_Short_Track_Speed_Skating_Championships "2003 World Short Track Speed Skating Championships"), [2009 UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/2009_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "2009 UCI Track Cycling World Championships"), [EuroBasket 2009](/wiki/EuroBasket_2009 "EuroBasket 2009"), [UEFA Euro 2012](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2012 "UEFA Euro 2012"), [2014 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship](/wiki/2014_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_World_Championship "2014 FIVB Volleyball Men's World Championship"), [2017 Men's European Volleyball Championship](/wiki/2017_Men%27s_European_Volleyball_Championship "2017 Men's European Volleyball Championship"), [2018 FIVB Volleyball Men's Club World Championship](/wiki/2018_FIVB_Volleyball_Men%27s_Club_World_Championship "2018 FIVB Volleyball Men's Club World Championship"), [2019 UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/2019_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "2019 UCI Track Cycling World Championships"), [2023 World Men's Handball Championship](/wiki/2023_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2023 World Men's Handball Championship").

[Deepspot](/wiki/Deepspot "Deepspot"), the world's second deepest swimming pool, is located in [Mszczonów](/wiki/Mszczon%C3%B3w "Mszczonów").

## Curiosities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Masovian_Voivodeship&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Curiosities")]

* The voivodeship contains both the current capital of Poland (Warsaw) and one of the former medieval capitals of Poland (Płock).
* In the 17th century, there were sizeable [Scottish](/wiki/Scots_in_Poland "Scots in Poland") communities in Warsaw, [Raciąż](/wiki/Raci%C4%85%C5%BC "Raciąż"), [Sierpc](/wiki/Sierpc "Sierpc") and [Zakroczym](/wiki/Zakroczym "Zakroczym"), and also smaller ones in [Iłża](/wiki/I%C5%82%C5%BCa "Iłża"), [Jedlińsk](/wiki/Jedli%C5%84sk "Jedlińsk"), [Radom](/wiki/Radom "Radom"), [Skrzynno](/wiki/Skrzynno,_Masovian_Voivodeship "Skrzynno, Masovian Voivodeship"), [Szydłowiec](/wiki/Szyd%C5%82owiec "Szydłowiec") and [Węgrów](/wiki/W%C4%99gr%C3%B3w "Węgrów").[[24]](#cite_note-25)
* The village of [Krasiniec](/wiki/Krasiniec "Krasiniec") was the home of the sole [Kalmyk](/wiki/Kalmyks "Kalmyks") community of Poland in the interbellum.
* One of the three parish churches of the [Armenian Catholic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Catholic_Church "Armenian Catholic Church") in Poland is located in Warsaw (see also: *[Armenians in Poland](/wiki/Armenians_in_Poland "Armenians in Poland")*).
